one_line_takeaway: MocklessTester is a Java unit-test generation system (not a security paper) that uses program-slicing-mined dependency-usage context plus a three-tier (symbol/protocol/iteration) constraint-checked repair loop to generate tests against real, unmocked dependencies, beating the PANTA baseline by roughly 20-25 percentage points on line/branch coverage across two benchmarks at a higher but practical token/time cost.
key_finding: On Defects4J, MocklessTester improves average line coverage from 68.83% to 88.82% (+19.99pp), branch coverage from 58.84% to 83.74% (+24.90pp), mutation score from 38.33% to 52.00% (+13.67pp), and project DepLC from 819 to 1197 lines (+378), while generating fewer tests on average than PANTA (402.79 vs 699.57). On Deps4J, it improves line coverage from 53.29% to 75.98% (+22.69pp), branch coverage from 42.34% to 58.12% (+15.78pp), and DepLC from 224 to 279 lines (+55), with mutation score roughly matching PANTA (47.09% vs 46.92%). All pooled improvements are statistically significant (p<0.05) with large effect sizes (|A12-0.5|>=0.21) except tokens/iteration (medium effect, A12=0.684). Efficiency (RQ2): MocklessTester costs more in total tokens/time than PANTA because it runs more iterations before stopping (mean 17.35 vs 7.07 iterations on Defects4J), but per-iteration it is cheaper in wall-clock time (254.60s vs 587.65s per iteration on Defects4J, a ~58.7% reduction) and only modestly more expensive per iteration in tokens (61.42k vs 50.17k). Absolute cost: 108.97s and 26.59k tokens per method on Defects4J, 69.85s and 25.46k tokens per method on Deps4J; the authors estimate $12-45 to run the full 14-project Defects4J benchmark using public API pricing. Ablation (RQ3): removing usage retrieval (-UR) causes the single largest drop in DepLC (1197->903 on Defects4J, 279->129 on Deps4J) as well as broad declines in line/branch/mutation metrics, indicating context-enriched generation is the dominant contributor; all four ablated variants underperform the full system with statistically significant, mostly large effect sizes (except two medium effects for -Sym on line/branch coverage).
core_contribution: The paper presents MocklessTester, an LLM-based Java unit test generation tool that generates tests exercising REAL dependency code instead of mocking dependencies (via Mockito/PowerMock). It targets two identified root causes of invalid generated tests: 'not knowing' (LLM lacks project-specific context about how to correctly instantiate/use dependency classes) and 'not following' (LLM fails to obey explicit constraints even when supplied). Two novel strategies are introduced as the paper's own contribution: (1) context-enriched generation, which uses a Joern code-property-graph program slicer to mine real dependency instantiation/usage patterns from the target project and inject them into the generation prompt; and (2) constraint-enforced fixing, a two-stage repair mechanism (Fixer I generates an initial repair from execution/compile feedback; Fixer II re-generates under explicit symbol-level constraints from a ClassIndex, protocol-level constraints from a Markov typestate model of valid API call orderings, and iteration-level constraints from an experience memory of gold tests/fix recipes/anti-patterns, and requires the model to produce a structured justification of how the fix satisfies these constraints). The system runs an iterative multi-agent plan-generate-validate-fix loop (Initializer, Planner, Generator, Validator, Fixer agents). The authors also construct a new benchmark, Deps4J, of 5 multi-module Java projects created after the backbone LLM's training cutoff, to avoid data-leakage concerns present in the older Defects4J benchmark, and introduce a new evaluation metric, DepLC (Dependency Line Coverage), which measures lines covered in dependency classes outside the class under test.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a software-engineering / automated-testing paper, not a security paper. There is no adversary, attacker capability, or security threat model; the object under study is the reliability/correctness of LLM-generated unit tests, not an attack or defense against malicious behavior.
limitations: The paper's own Threats to Validity section notes: (1) Construct validity - standard coverage/mutation metrics only partially capture test quality, mitigated by using multiple complementary metrics including the new DepLC. (2) Internal validity - results depend on the single backbone LLM (Qwen3-Coder-30B) and identical-but-fixed hyperparameters shared with the baseline; benchmarking across different LLMs was out of scope. (3) Conclusion validity - LLM stochasticity means results are single-run averages per project rather than multi-run repeats, due to the high per-iteration cost (tens of thousands of tokens and minutes per iteration) of repeating the already-expensive evaluation. (4) External validity - evaluation is limited to Java projects and one backbone LLM; generalization to other languages/models is untested. The paper also reports (in RQ1 discussion) a notable failure case: on the JQuick project (a cURL-to-HTTP parser), both MocklessTester and PANTA perform far below their averages (MocklessTester: 60.34% line / 30.29% branch coverage vs 75.98%/58.12% Deps4J averages) because parser-centric code requires grammar-valid inputs that neither tool can construct from coarse-grained parse-failure feedback; the authors suggest combining with grammar-aware/fuzzing input generation as future work.

datasets_used: Defects4J v2.0.0 (14 Java projects, 130 non-trivial classes with cyclomatic complexity >=11, 2971 methods under test, Java 11/JUnit 4, commits 2008-2018)
Deps4J (new benchmark introduced by this paper: 5 projects - adk-java, fit-framework, agentscope-java, a2a-java, jquick-curl - 30 classes, 540 methods, Java 17/JUnit 5, created after 2025)
quantitative_results: Defects4J average line coverage: MocklessTester 88.82% vs PANTA 68.83% (+19.99pp)
Defects4J average branch coverage: MocklessTester 83.74% vs PANTA 58.84% (+24.90pp)
Defects4J average mutation score: MocklessTester 52.00% vs PANTA 38.33% (+13.67pp)
Defects4J average project DepLC: MocklessTester 1197 vs PANTA 819 lines (+378)
Deps4J average line coverage: MocklessTester 75.98% vs PANTA 53.29% (+22.69pp)
Deps4J average branch coverage: MocklessTester 58.12% vs PANTA 42.34% (+15.78pp)
Deps4J average mutation score: MocklessTester 47.09% vs PANTA 46.92% (+0.17pp, roughly tied)
Deps4J average project DepLC: MocklessTester 279 vs PANTA 224 lines (+55)
Defects4J efficiency: MocklessTester 108.97s and 26.59k tokens per method vs PANTA 58.05s and 5.74k tokens per method
Deps4J efficiency: MocklessTester 69.85s and 25.46k tokens per method vs PANTA 43.57s and 9.73k tokens per method
Estimated cost to run MocklessTester on full Defects4J (14 projects): $12-45 using public API pricing
Ablation -UR (no usage retrieval) drop in DepLC: 1197->903 (Defects4J), 279->129 (Deps4J), the largest ablation effect on both datasets
baselines_compared: PANTA (prior SOTA LLM-based Java test generator using path-based prompting; the paper's primary, directly-compared baseline)

CANDOR (first multi-agent LLM-based Java test generation framework, cited as related work/prior art but limited to method-level testing without dependencies; not directly re-run as an experimental baseline in this paper's tables)
EvoSuite and Randoop (traditional search-based/random-based test generators, mentioned as background/related work, not included in the paper's own experimental comparison tables)
